# Verify `sesam user regen-sign-key` rotates a user's signing key: the new key
# is written to disk, recorded in the audit log, and is usable end-to-end (the
# user can still sign operations with it).

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Add bob as an admin so he can sign admin operations himself.
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --group admin
exists .sesam/signkeys/bob.age

# Regenerate bob's signing key.
exec sesam user regen-sign-key --user bob

# The audit log records the operation...
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.regenerate_sign_key"'
stdout '"bob"'

# ...and `sesam log` renders it.
exec sesam log
stdout 'regenerated signing key of bob'

exec sesam verify

# End-to-end: bob signs a new operation with his regenerated key. This only
# succeeds if the on-disk signing key matches the pubkey recorded by the regen
# entry - it would fail on any encode/decode or key mismatch.
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY tell --user carol --recipient $CAROL_PUBKEY --group dev
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"carol"'
exec sesam verify

# Regenerating the key of a non-existent user fails.
! exec sesam user regen-sign-key --user ghost
